revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:آسوکا]][[ja:飛鳥]][[zh:飛鳥 (地名)]]
'''Asuka''' (飛鳥) is a historic region formerly located in {{nihongo|Yamato Province|Yamato Province |Yamato no Kuni|qq=}}, now part of modern-day {{nihongo|Asuka Village|明日香村|Asuka-mura|qq=}}, in {{nihongo|Nara Prefecture|奈良県|Nara-ken|qq=}}. It is renowned for its cultural and political significance during the {{nihongo|Asuka period|飛鳥時代|Asuka Jidai|qq=}}, which spanned from the late 6th century to the early 8th century.
The region also has a namesake in {{nihongo|Kawachi Province|河内国|Kawachi no Kuni|qq=}}, located in present-day {{nihongo|Habikino City|羽曳野市|Habikino-shi|qq=}} and {{nihongo|Taishi Town|太子町|Taishi-chō|qq=}} in Osaka Prefecture. To differentiate between the two, the Nara Asuka is often called "Distant Asuka" (遠つ飛鳥, ''Tōtsu Asuka''), while the Osaka Asuka is referred to as "Nearby Asuka" (近つ飛鳥, ''Chikatsu Asuka''). == Etymology == The name "Asuka" is subject to various theories, including foreign origin and geographic references: * '''Foreign origin theories:''' ** Linked to Sanskrit words like ''Ashoka'' or ''Asoka'' (a type of tree or emperor's name). ** Derived from ancient Korean (Buyeo) language where "Ska" means "village." ** Traced to continental place names such as Anshuku (安宿). * '''Geographical references:''' ** Refers to a shallow river ("Asu-ka" as shallow water or land). ** Indicates a "place of collapse" or erosion. The kanji representation of "飛鳥" (flying bird) may derive from poetic metaphors found in the {{nihongo|Man'yōshū|万葉集|qq=}}, the oldest anthology of Japanese poetry. == Historical Significance == Asuka was a central hub of political and cultural activity during the Asuka period. It housed several imperial palaces and served as the nation's political center. The period saw the introduction of Buddhism, advanced architectural techniques, and the establishment of the Taika Reforms, which laid the foundations for the Japanese state. === Palaces in Asuka === Notable imperial palaces in the Asuka region include: - {{nihongo|Toyura Palace|豊浦宮|Toyura no Miya|qq=}} (Empress Suiko) - {{nihongo|Okamoto Palace|岡本宮|Okamoto no Miya|qq=}} (Emperor Jomei) - {{nihongo|Asuka Kiyomihara Palace|飛鳥浄御原宮|Asuka no Kiyomihara no Miya|qq=}} (Emperor Tenmu and Empress Jitō) == Archaeological Discoveries == The region is famous for its rich archaeological heritage, including: - {{nihongo|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|高松塚古墳|Takamatsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} - {{nihongo|Asuka Temple|飛鳥寺|Asuka-dera|qq=}}, one of the oldest Buddhist temples in Japan. - Numerous stone monuments and artifacts, such as the {{nihongo|Saruishi|猿石|Monkey Stone|qq=}} and the {{nihongo|Ishibutai Kofun|石舞台古墳|Ishibutai Kofun|qq=}}. == Cultural and Modern Significance == Asuka remains a symbol of Japan's early cultural and political development. Today, it is preserved as part of the {{nihongo|Asuka Historical National Government Park|国営飛鳥歴史公園|Kokuei Asuka Rekishi Kōen|qq=}}, drawing visitors interested in history, archaeology, and traditional landscapes.
== See Also == - {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} - {{ill|Asuka-Fujiwara|en|Asuka-Fujiwara|qid=Q4811849|lt=Asuka-Fujiwara archaeological sites|1=Asuka-Fujiwara}} - {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|qid=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} - {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} - {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|qid=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum|1=Asuka Historical Museum}} == References == {{Reflist|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|飛鳥|version=101210045|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:飛鳥]]
